text: The surgeon begins medial dissection of the left colon, identifying the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) as a key anatomical landmark.
The surgeon performs dissection towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), carefully preserving the sympathetic nerves on both sides of the aorta.
The surgeon meticulously dissects around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), carefully navigating the surrounding tissue to preserve anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), completing the dissection around this vascular structure.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection superiorly towards the splenic flexure, advancing the dissection plane along the colon.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), completing the vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the splenic flexure following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), carefully navigating the tissue planes.
The surgeon identifies the tail of the pancreas and continues dissection along the pancreatic border, gradually opening the lesser sac.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, revealing the underlying anatomical structures as the dissection progresses along the pancreatic border.
The surgeon initiates superior dissection of the left colon by carefully opening the omentum, revealing underlying structures and facilitating further access.
The surgeon continues dissection along the omentum, progressing towards the splenic pole, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the left colon following medial and superior mobilization, continuing the dissection towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon begins dissection from the iliac fossa, progressing upwards towards the spleen, following the anatomical pathway along the left colon.
The surgeon demonstrates the complete mobilization of the splenic flexure and left colon, with visible separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs additional pelvic dissection to ensure adequate distal margins, focusing on the lower part of the surgical field.
The surgeon transects the rectum, completing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection along the plane between the retroperitoneal fascia and the parietal peritoneum, further separating these tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, visibly separating the pancreas and duodenum from the mesocolon.
Following the lateral dissection, the surgeon performs medial dissection of the right colon, carefully separating it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon begins medial dissection of the right colon, identifying the superior mesenteric and ileocolic vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the ileocolic vessels, securing them to prevent bleeding during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection superiorly following the ligation of the ileocolic vessels, advancing the dissection along the right colon.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic vessels, gastrocolic trunk, ASPDV, and accessory right colic vein during further medial dissection of the right colon.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the middle colic artery and vein during the medial dissection of the right colon.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the accessory right colic vein at the level just before the gastroepiploic confluence.
The surgeon continues the dissection toward the lesser sac, carefully separating the mesocolon from the head and neck of the pancreas.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac and separates the pancreas head from the mesocollum, revealing the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the right colon by elevating it superiorly, further freeing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs dissection along the omental opening, continuing from the left colon mobilization, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the total colonic mobilization, ensuring the colon is fully freed from its attachments.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a mini laparotomy by extending the umbilical port, visibly removing the tissue from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon checks the alignment of the mesentery to ensure there is no internal hernia or kinking of the small bowel, visually inspecting the positioning and tension of the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end ileorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the ileum to the rectum with precise alignment.
The surgeon inserts air through the anus to check for leaks in the newly formed anastomosis, observing for any visible air bubbles or fluid escape.
The surgeon inserts a Jackson Pratt drain posteriorly to the anastomosis site, ensuring proper drainage from the surgical area.
